Make a compelling case, and then stop talking. The silence after a strong argument is not awkward.
We phase out the old performance cycle over two quarters. We do not switch it off in one.
Flexible work is a double-edged sword: it widens the talent pool and it weakens the informal culture.
Never had she seen a plan survive because it was right.
Not only did we redesign the performance cycle, but we also rebuilt the onboarding.
Hardly had we launched the pilot when the engagement scores began to rise.
Never have I seen a people strategy succeed without an owner in the room.
Before the board joins, give me the first line of your presentation. Not the plan. The line.

What the data shows us is that one senior departure costs us eleven months of hiring, and we have had four this year. And I would like to draw your attention to the second line of the dashboard: succession planning, two of nine critical roles.
Good. Now somebody in that room will tell you that talent retention is a lovely problem to have in a year when we are cutting budgets. What do you say?

I would not argue with the budget. Never have I seen a people strategy win that argument. I would put the cost of doing nothing next to the cost of the plan, in people analytics, and let them read both.
And the flexible model? Two of the directors will tell you it is destroying the culture they spent twenty years building.

They are half right, and I intend to say so before they do. Flexible work is a double-edged sword: not only did it widen our talent pool, but it also weakened the informal culture that used to do the work for us. That is change management, and I would rather manage it than deny it.
What the data shows us is that one senior departure costs us eleven months of hiring.
I would like to draw your attention to our succession coverage: two of nine critical roles.
Flexible work is a double-edged sword, and I would rather manage it than deny it.
What this means in practice is one owner per pillar, one metric, and a review in ninety days.

Danielle, it is Claire. Your slot with the board is on Thursday, and I want you to walk in knowing exactly what you are walking into. You have no authority in that room. Not one of those five people reports to you, and two of them have been running their region since before Maple Bear had a Canadian office. So the first thing to understand is that nothing gets adopted because you presented it. It gets adopted because somebody in that room decides to carry it. The second thing. They will not argue with your people strategy. They will argue with what it costs. Somebody will say that talent retention is a lovely problem to have in a year when we are cutting budgets, and if you answer that with values, you lose the room. Answer it with people analytics. Bring the number that says what one senior departure costs us, and put it next to the cost of the plan. And Danielle, do not soften it. Make a compelling case, and then stop talking. The silence after a strong argument is not awkward. It is the sound of five people deciding.

Danielle, a quick note before Thursday. I have watched a lot of people present to this board, and the ones who fail all fail in the same way. They present a plan. This board does not adopt plans. It adopts arguments. So, three things. Open with what the data shows, never with what you believe. The moment you say I feel, you become a consultant, and consultants get thanked and then ignored. Second, name the trade-off before somebody else names it for you. Every people strategy is a double-edged sword. Flexible work widens the talent pool and it weakens the informal culture, and if you pretend otherwise, one of the directors will say it out loud, and then it is his point and not yours. Third, when they push on cost, do not defend your budget. Give them the cost of doing nothing. Not only did our last two departures cost us eleven months of hiring, but they also took the succession plan with them. Say that sentence, and watch the room change.
